title: W8: DDP, FSDP, and Efficient Attention
blurb: High priority for calculation-heavy computing questions. Summer 2025 asks DDP communication and FlashAttention directly.

knowledge_points.title: DDP Gradient Synchronization
knowledge_points.recap: DDP synchronizes gradients after backward, commonly via reduce-scatter plus all-gather. Be able to reason about chunks, ranks, and communication cost.

questions.q:
Consider distributed data parallel training of a large language model using $r^2$ GPUs, where $r$ is an integer. The GPUs are connected in a 2D mesh topology. The model has $N=r^2m$ parameters and each GPU holds a local gradient vector of size $N$ after the backward pass. Gradients are synchronized using a reduce-scatter followed by an all-gather. Each communication has a startup cost $T_s$ and a per-element transmission time $T_m$.

1. Derive the total communication time for a reduce-scatter operation using the ring algorithm as discussed in class. Express your answer in terms of $r,m,T_s,T_m$. (7 points)
questions.answer:
We can do reduce-scatter on a mesh topology in **two phases**, each with $r-1$ iterations **(2 points)**.

**Phase 1: column-wise reduce-scatter.** Each column has $r$ ranks. Each rank holds $r^2m$ elements in total, so each column is responsible for reducing $r^2m/r=rm$ elements **(1 point)**. Each rank participates in reduce-scatter across $r$ ranks over $rm$ data. The communication cost per rank is

$$(r-1)(T_s+rmT_m)\quad\mathbf{(1\ point)}.$$

**Phase 2: row-wise reduce-scatter.** After phase 1, each rank holds $rm$ elements **(1 point)**. Each row performs reduce-scatter across $r$ ranks, reducing the data further to $m$ elements per rank. The cost is

$$(r-1)(T_s+mT_m)\quad\mathbf{(1\ point)}.$$

The total cost is

$$\mathbf{(r-1)(2T_s+(r+1)mT_m)}\quad\mathbf{(1\ point)}.$$

The exam also accepted an inefficient ring over all $r^2$ ranks with cost $(r^2-1)(T_s+mT_m)$ if the mesh path/partners and chunking were clearly identified.
questions.extend: Memory: two-phase mesh reduce-scatter = column over $rm$, then row over $m$. Add both costs.

questions.q: Supplemental practice (not a direct exam question): Why does Distributed Data Parallel training need gradient synchronization?
questions.answer: Each model replica sees a different mini-batch, so its local gradient is only a partial estimate of the global batch gradient. **Gradient synchronization sums or averages gradients across replicas**, making the replicas perform the same parameter update.
questions.extend: Memory: DDP replicates parameters but splits data; gradients must be synchronized so replicas stay identical.

questions.q: Tutorial 9. In recursive halving with $P=2^d$ GPUs, each iteration flips one bit of the rank. In iteration 2, where do rank 0 and rank $P/2$ send data? What are broadcast and all-reduce costs for gradient length $N$ with latency $T_s$ and per-element time $T_g$?
questions.answer:
In iteration 2, rank 0 sends to **$P/4$**, and rank $P/2$ sends to **$3P/4$**.

Broadcast takes $\log_2P$ rounds, each sending $N$ elements:

$$T_{broad}=\log_2P(T_s+NT_g).$$

All-reduce is reduce plus broadcast, so

$$T_{allreduce}=2\log_2P(T_s+NT_g).$$
questions.extend: Memory: recursive halving is logarithmic rounds with full-size messages.

questions.q: Tutorial 9. Compare ring all-reduce with ring reduce-scatter plus all-gather. Derive costs in terms of $P,N,T_s,T_g$, and give the speedup when $T_s\approx0$.
questions.answer:
Ring all-reduce uses $P-1$ iterations with full gradient messages:

$$T_{ring}=(P-1)(T_s+NT_g).$$

Reduce-scatter plus all-gather has two phases of $P-1$ iterations, but messages are size $N/P$:

$$T_{RS+AG}=2(P-1)\left(T_s+\frac{N}{P}T_g\right).$$

When $T_s\approx0$, the speedup is

$$\frac{T_{ring}}{T_{RS+AG}}=\frac{P}{2}.$$
questions.extend: Memory: reduce-scatter/all-gather reduces bandwidth by sending chunks.

knowledge_points.title: FSDP/ZeRO and FlashAttention
knowledge_points.recap: FSDP and ZeRO reduce memory by sharding states. FlashAttention speeds attention by reducing memory traffic rather than changing the mathematical result.

questions.q: 1. Explain the two main techniques used by Flash Attention to speed up the computation of attention. (2 points)
questions.answer:
**Tiling:** The large input matrices $Q,K,V$, stored in slow high-bandwidth memory, are partitioned into smaller blocks that are small enough to be loaded into the GPU's fast on-chip SRAM **(1 point)**.

**Operator fusion:** The steps of attention calculation, score computation, softmax, and multiplication by value matrix $V$, are merged into a single computational kernel. This fused kernel executes all operations on tiles loaded in fast on-chip SRAM **without writing large intermediate matrices back to slow HBM (1 point)**.
questions.extend: Memory: FlashAttention changes memory traffic, not the mathematical attention result.

questions.q: In the ZeRO-2 memory-accounting setting above, what does ZeRO-2 shard and what does it keep replicated?
questions.answer:
In **ZeRO-2, gradients and optimizer states are sharded across GPUs**, while each GPU still stores **the full forward/backward weights**. In the WS26 memory formula this gives per-parameter per-GPU memory

$$2+\frac{14}{G}\text{ bytes},$$

where the 2 bytes are replicated BF16 weights and the 14 bytes are sharded gradients plus FP32 optimizer states.
questions.extend: Memory: ZeRO-1 shards optimizer states; ZeRO-2 shards optimizer states + gradients; parameters remain replicated.

questions.q: FlashAttention tiling problem. Let $A\in\mathbb{R}^{M\times K}$, $B\in\mathbb{R}^{K\times N}$, and $C=AB$, partitioned into $2\times2$ blocks. Starting from $[C]_{pq}=\sum_{k=1}^K a_{pk}b_{kq}$, show $C_{ij}=\sum_{l=1}^2 A_{il}B_{lj}$.
questions.answer:
Split the inner dimension $K=K_1+K_2$. For any entry in block $C_{ij}$,

$$[C]_{pq}=\sum_{k=1}^{K_1}a_{pk}b_{kq}+\sum_{k=K_1+1}^{K}a_{pk}b_{kq}.$$

The first sum is the entry of **$A_{i1}B_{1j}$**, and the second is the entry of **$A_{i2}B_{2j}$**. Therefore

$$C_{ij}=A_{i1}B_{1j}+A_{i2}B_{2j}=\sum_{l=1}^{2}A_{il}B_{lj}.$$
questions.extend: Memory: tiling just splits the scalar inner sum by block boundary.

questions.q:
FlashAttention exactness problem. Prove by induction that the online recurrence

$$O_i^{(j)}=\frac{d_{j-1}e^{m_{j-1}-m_j}}{d_j}O_i^{(j-1)}+\frac{e^{s_{ij}-m_j}}{d_j}v_j$$

computes $O_i^{(j)}=\frac{\sum_{k=1}^{j}e^{s_{ik}-m_j}v_k}{d_j}$ and hence exact attention.
questions.answer:
Base case $j=1$: $m_1=s_{i1}$ and $d_1=1$, so $O_i^{(1)}=v_1$.

Inductive step: substitute

$$O_i^{(j-1)}=\frac{\sum_{k=1}^{j-1}e^{s_{ik}-m_{j-1}}v_k}{d_{j-1}}$$

into the recurrence. The rescaling factor $e^{m_{j-1}-m_j}$ converts old terms to the new maximum $m_j$, giving

$$O_i^{(j)}=\frac{\sum_{k=1}^{j}e^{s_{ik}-m_j}v_k}{d_j}.$$

At $j=N$, $m_N$ and $d_N$ are the exact softmax max and denominator, so the result is **exact attention**.
questions.extend: Memory: online softmax is exact because old accumulators are rescaled when the running max changes.

questions.q: Homework 8 code-understanding. The naive CUDA matrix multiplication kernel uses a 2D grid. Each thread computes one output element. What should the kernel do?
questions.answer:
Each thread computes global `(row, col)` from block and thread indices, checks bounds, and computes

$$C[row,col]=\sum_k A[row,k]B[k,col].$$

The key points are **2D indexing**, **bounds checks**, and a loop over the inner dimension. It is correct but slow because all values are repeatedly read from global memory.
questions.extend: Memory: naive CUDA matmul = one thread per output element.

questions.q: Homework 8 code-understanding. The optimized CUDA matrix multiplication uses shared memory. Why does shared memory help, and what must the tiled kernel do?
questions.answer:
Shared memory helps because threads in a block reuse the same tiles of $A$ and $B$. Instead of repeatedly loading from global memory, threads cooperatively load tiles into **on-chip shared memory**, synchronize, multiply-accumulate from the tiles, and repeat over the inner dimension.

The kernel must allocate shared tiles, load valid elements with boundary checks, call synchronization before use, accumulate partial dot products, and handle edge tiles.
questions.extend: Memory: shared-memory matmul reduces global memory traffic by tile reuse.
